content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

After learning HTTP, REST APIs, controllers, services, repositories, and databases, I reached a concept that quietly powers almost every high-performance system:
Caching.
At first, caching sounds simple:
Store something so you can reuse it later.
But once I started learning it from a backend engineering perspective, I realized caching is everywhere:

• Google Search

• Netflix streaming

• Twitter/X trending topics

• DNS resolution

• CDNs

• Redis

• API rate limiting

• Sessions

• Database query optimization
Caching is one of those concepts that looks small but has massive impact.
It can reduce latency, decrease database load, save compute, reduce external API costs, and make systems feel instant.

What is Caching?
In one sentence:
Caching is a mechanism that reduces the time and effort required to retrieve data or perform work.
A more technical definition:
Caching means storing a frequently used subset of data in a faster-access location so future requests can be served more quickly.
The important words are:
Subset
Faster Access
Future RequestsCaching does not mean copying your entire database.
It means choosing useful data and storing it somewhere faster.

The First-Principles Problem
Imagine your backend has to answer the same expensive question again and again.
Example:
Get dashboard analytics for a userTo answer this, your server may need to:

• Query millions of rows

• Join multiple tables

• Run aggregations

• Format the response

• Send it back to the client
Now imagine thousands of users requesting this repeatedly.
Without caching:
Request
↓
Expensive Computation
↓
Database
↓
ResponseEvery request repeats the work.
With caching:
Request
↓
Check Cache
↓
Return Cached ResultThe expensive work happens only when needed.

Why Caching Matters
Caching helps with three major things:
1. Lower Latency
Users get responses faster.
2. Lower Server Load
The backend performs less repeated work.
3. Lower Cost
Fewer database queries, fewer external API calls, and less compute usage.
Caching is not just a performance trick.
It is a system design strategy.

Real-World Example 1: Google Search
Google processes billions of search queries.
Some queries are repeated constantly.
Example:
weather todayWithout caching, Google would need to recompute search results for every repeated query.
That means:

• More CPU usage

• More memory usage

• Higher latency

• More server load
Instead, Google can cache popular query results.
Flow:
User searches query
↓
Check cache
↓
Cache hit?
↓
Return instantlyIf the result is not cached:
Run expensive search workflow
↓
Compute result
↓
Store in cache
↓
Return responseThis is the first important caching pattern:
If something is expensive to compute and requested often, cache it.

Cache Hit and Cache Miss
Two terms are important.
Cache Hit
The requested data is found in cache.
Request
↓
Cache
↓
Data FoundFast path.

Cache Miss
The requested data is not found in cache.
Request
↓
Cache
↓
Data Not Found
↓
Fetch from source
↓
Store in cacheSlower path.
The goal of caching is to increase cache hits while keeping data reasonably fresh.

Real-World Example 2: Netflix and CDN Caching
Netflix streams massive video files to users all over the world.
If every user had to fetch video directly from Netflix’s origin servers, users far away would experience high latency and buffering.
So Netflix uses CDN-style caching.
CDN means:
Content Delivery NetworkA CDN stores copies of content on servers closer to users.
These servers are called:
Edge Serversor:
Edge LocationsExample:
Origin Server in US
↓
Edge Server in India
↓
User in IndiaInstead of sending every request across the world, Netflix serves content from a nearby edge server.
This reduces:

• Latency

• Buffering

• Origin server load

• Bandwidth cost
The key idea:
Move data closer to where it will be used.

Real-World Example 3: Twitter/X Trending Topics
Trending topics require heavy computation.
A platform like Twitter/X must analyze huge volumes of posts, hashtags, regions, time windows, and engagement patterns.
If it recalculated trends every time a user opened the trending tab, the system would collapse.
Instead:
Compute trends periodically
↓
Store result in cache
↓
Serve users from cacheThis works because trending topics do not change every millisecond.
They can safely be cached for a short duration.
This gives us another caching principle:
Cache data that changes slowly but is read frequently.

When Should You Use Caching?
Caching is useful when data is:

• Expensive to compute

• Expensive to fetch

• Read frequently

• Updated infrequently

• Safe to reuse temporarily
Good examples:

• Product details

• User profiles

• Dashboard statistics

• Search results

• API responses

• Sessions

• Rate limit counters

• Static assets

• DNS records
Bad examples:

• Bank balance after every transaction

• Real-time stock price without freshness rules

• Highly personalized sensitive data

• Data that changes constantly
Caching is powerful, but wrong caching can create stale or incorrect data.

Levels of Caching
As backend engineers, caching appears at multiple levels.
The common ones are:
Network-Level Caching
Hardware-Level Caching
Software-Level Caching
1. Network-Level Caching
Network caching improves data delivery across the internet.
Two major examples:

• CDN caching

• DNS caching

CDN Caching
A CDN caches static or semi-static content near users.
Examples:

• Images

• Videos

• CSS files

• JavaScript files

• HTML pages

• Downloadable assets
Flow:
User requests image
↓
Nearest CDN edge server
↓
Cache hit?
↓
Return imageIf cache miss:
Edge server fetches from origin
↓
Stores copy
↓
Returns responseThis is used by:

• Netflix

• YouTube

• Vercel

• Cloudflare

• Amazon CloudFront

CDN Terms
Origin Server
The original source of truth.
Edge Server
A nearby server that caches content.
Point of Presence (PoP)
A geographical location containing multiple edge servers.
TTL
Time To Live.
It defines how long cached content should stay valid.
Example:
Cache this image for 1 hour.After TTL expires, the CDN fetches fresh content again.

DNS Caching
DNS converts domain names into IP addresses.
Example:
example.com
↓
93.184.216.34Without DNS caching, every browser request would need to repeatedly resolve the domain from scratch.
DNS resolution can involve:

• Browser cache

• Operating system cache

• Recursive resolver

• Root servers

• TLD servers

• Authoritative name servers
Caching happens at multiple levels to avoid repeating this process.
DNS caching makes browsing faster.

2. Hardware-Level Caching
Caching also exists inside computers.
CPUs use multiple levels of cache:
L1 Cache
L2 Cache
L3 Cache
RAM
DiskCloser to CPU means faster but smaller.
Farther from CPU means slower but larger.
This is why accessing data from memory is faster than disk.

RAM vs Disk
RAM
Fast but volatile.
If power goes off:
Data goneDisk
Slower but persistent.
If power goes off:
Data survivesThis difference explains why in-memory databases are fast.

3. Software-Level Caching
This is the caching backend engineers work with most often.
Examples:

• Redis

• Memcached

• AWS ElastiCache
These are commonly called:
In-memory key-value stores
Why Redis is Fast
Redis stores data primarily in RAM.
RAM access is much faster than disk access.
Traditional databases like PostgreSQL store durable data on disk, while Redis is optimized for fast access.
This is why Redis is commonly used for:

• Sessions

• Rate limits

• Cached API responses

• Temporary counters

• Frequently accessed data

Redis as a Key-Value Store
Redis stores data like this:
key → valueExample:
user:123 → {"name": "Shubham"}To retrieve:
GET user:123To store:
SET user:123 valueSimple, fast, and effective.

Caching Strategies
There are multiple ways to use cache.
Two common backend strategies are:
Cache-Aside / Lazy Caching
Write-Through Caching
Cache-Aside / Lazy Caching
This is one of the most common patterns.
Flow:
Request comes in
↓
Check cache
↓
If found, return
↓
If not found, query database
↓
Store result in cache
↓
Return responseThis is called lazy caching because data is cached only when requested.
Example:
GET /products/123First request:
Cache miss
↓
Fetch from DB
↓
Store in Redis
↓
Return responseSecond request:
Cache hit
↓
Return from RedisThis pattern is useful when you don’t know in advance what users will request.

Write-Through Caching
In write-through caching, the cache is updated whenever the database is updated.
Example:
Update product in database
↓
Update product in cache
↓
Return responseAdvantage:
Cache stays freshDisadvantage:
Writes become slowerbecause every write updates both database and cache.

Cache Invalidation
One of the hardest problems in caching is invalidation.
Invalidation means:
Removing or updating cached data when it becomes stale.
Example:
Product price changes.
Cached value:
₹1000New database value:
₹900If cache is not invalidated, users see the wrong price.
Common invalidation approaches:

• Delete cache on update

• Update cache on update

• Use TTL

• Use versioned cache keys
Caching is easy when data never changes.
Caching becomes hard when freshness matters.

Eviction Policies
Cache memory is limited.
Eventually, the cache becomes full.
The system must decide what to remove.
This is called eviction.

No Eviction
If memory is full, new writes fail.
Not usually ideal.

LRU: Least Recently Used
Remove the item that was accessed least recently.
Example:
A used today
B used today
C used yesterdayRemove:
CUseful when recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again.

LFU: Least Frequently Used
Remove the item used least often.
Example:
A accessed 100 times
B accessed 5 times
C accessed 1 timeRemove:
CUseful when frequency matters more than recency.

TTL-Based Eviction
Items expire after a configured duration.
Example:
weather:mumbai → expires in 1 hourAfter one hour, Redis removes it automatically.
TTL is extremely useful for data that can be stale temporarily but should refresh eventually.

Backend Use Case 1: Database Query Caching
Suppose a dashboard API runs a heavy query:
Multiple joins
Aggregations
Millions of rowsIf this API is called frequently, it can overload the database.
Solution:
Cache query result for 5 minutesFlow:
Check Redis
↓
If found, return
↓
If not, query Postgres
↓
Store in Redis
↓
ReturnThis reduces database load significantly.

Backend Use Case 2: Product Details
E-commerce websites often cache product data.
Why?
Product descriptions, images, and metadata do not change every second.
Example:
MacBook product pageDuring a sale, millions of users may request the same page.
Caching prevents every request from hitting the database.

Backend Use Case 3: User Profiles
Social media platforms often cache profile data.
A celebrity profile may be viewed millions of times.
But the profile itself changes rarely.
Perfect cache candidate.

Backend Use Case 4: Sessions
In session-based authentication, every authenticated request requires session lookup.
If sessions were stored only in PostgreSQL, every API request would hit the database.
Instead, sessions are commonly stored in Redis.
Why?

• Fast lookup

• Temporary data

• Easy expiration

• Lower database load

Backend Use Case 5: External API Caching
Imagine your backend calls a weather API.
If 10,000 users ask for the same city weather, you don’t want to call the external API 10,000 times.
External APIs often have:

• Rate limits

• Pricing

• Latency
Solution:
Cache weather response for 1 hourThis saves money and improves speed.

Backend Use Case 6: Rate Limiting
Rate limiting protects APIs from abuse.
Example rule:
50 requests per minute per IPRedis can store counters:
rate_limit:192.168.1.1 → 32Each request increments the counter.
If count exceeds limit:
429 Too Many RequestsRedis is perfect here because counters need to be fast and temporary.

Caching Tradeoffs
Caching is not free.
It
